↑
ML Interview Prep · Session Reference

Information, Entropy & Decision Trees

A complete, ordered reconstruction of everything worked through in this session — from what "information" fundamentally is, through entropy, cross-entropy, KL and the losses they generate, down to how a decision tree is actually built, optimized, and made tractable.

Compiled for Samarth · all derivations & math preserved

Contents

  1. What Information Actually Is
  2. Surprise & Information: One Quantity
  3. Why the Entropy Formula Is What It Is
  4. Entropy: Definition, Shape, Units
  5. Cross-Entropy
  6. KL Divergence
  7. MLE, Log-Loss & "Are We Optimizing Entropy?"
  8. Knowledge Distillation (KD) Loss
  9. Gini Impurity & Its Link to Entropy
  10. Information Gain & Gini Gain
  11. How a Decision Tree Is Built
  12. Splitting: Continuous & Categorical Features
  13. Stopping, Pruning & Leaf Predictions
  14. Classification → Regression Trees
  15. Complexity: Training & Inference
  16. ID3 / C4.5 / CART & Library Reality
  17. Categorical Tricks: Breiman & CatBoost
  18. Consolidated Q&A / Checks

01What Information Actually Is

Shannon "information" has nothing to do with meaning, facts, or content. He deliberately threw meaning away. It is a count of something physical.

Core definition The information content of an outcome is the number of optimal yes/no questions needed to identify it. A bit is one yes/no question's worth — a count of questions, not a substance.

The concrete game

Pick one of 8 equally likely items. Identify it with yes/no questions by halving the possibilities each time: 8 → 4 → 2 → 1. Always exactly 3 questions, and . The log is literally counting halvings: to go from candidates to 1 you need halvings.

So an outcome of probability carries bits. Rarer outcome → smaller → more halvings → more questions → more information. A near-certain event () needs ~0 questions — you already know.

Why "bits" — storage and transmission

Encode each yes/no answer as one binary digit (yes = 1, no = 0). Three answers form a 3-digit code like 101; codes cover 8 items exactly. So three phrasings are the same number:

  • 3 yes/no questions to identify it,
  • 3 binary digits to store / transmit it,
  • bits of information.

"This cost me 3 bits" = "an optimal questioner needs 3 questions / I must physically write 3 binary digits to pin it down." This is why we care: it is the answer to how much memory and how much bandwidth a message consumes.

What is information? Reduction of uncertainty, measured by how many binary choices it takes to remove it. A message is informative precisely when it rules out many possibilities. Not meaning — uncertainty removed.

02Surprise & Information: One Quantity, Two Viewpoints

The block that confused us: surprise and information aren't two things to "fit together" — they're the same number viewed before vs. after the event.

Take one outcome with probability . The quantity has two readings:

ViewpointNameQuestion
Before it happens (forward)Surprise"How shocked will I be if this occurs?"
After it happens (backward)Information"How much did learning this resolve?"

They are forced to be equal because both equal "how many possibilities did this outcome eliminate." A low-probability outcome was hiding among many alternatives — so it is both surprising (you didn't expect this one out of many) and informative (learning it killed all the others). High surprise ⟺ many possibilities ruled out ⟺ high information. They cannot come apart.

Mental model to lock in Not "related" — identical, named for whether you stand before the event (surprise) or after it (information).

03Why the Entropy Formula Is What It Is

The honest derivation — not "a function that does these three things is ," but a theorem that forces it uniquely.

The one real axiom: independent surprises add

For independent events with probabilities and , surprise should satisfy . Why additivity?

  • Information accumulates linearly. Ten independent coin flips carry ten times the information of one — no overlap, because independent. Linear scaling with count means it adds, not multiplies. If surprise multiplied, two flips would be the square of one flip's surprise — nonsense as "amount learned."
  • It bridges two worlds. Probabilities of independent events multiply (); we want information to add. We need a map from the multiplicative world of probabilities to the additive world of information. That requirement is the axiom.

The theorem: additivity + continuity forces a logarithm

Want on with (1) and (2) continuous (or merely monotonic / locally bounded — very weak).

Claim: the only solutions are , .

Proof sketch (Cauchy's functional equation). Let for . With , additivity becomes

The only continuous (or monotonic / locally bounded) solutions are linear: . Translate back: . ∎

So you genuinely cannot have any other function. Monotonicity ("rarer = more surprising") just pins . And is a consequence, not a separate axiom: .

Why the constant in front doesn't matter

There is nothing forcing . The constant is real and free — it is a choice of units, exactly like meters vs. feet:

Constant / baseUnit
(i.e. )bits
(natural log)nats
bans / dits (historical)
Why nobody justifies the base Every decision and gradient is invariant to . In a tree, multiplying all entropies by multiplies Information Gain by — the argmax is unchanged. In training, loss × constant has gradient × constant, absorbed by the learning rate. The base washes out of everything that matters. Base 2 for the clean "bits = questions" story; natural log for clean calculus.
The honest logical structure:
  1. Axiom: information from independent events adds.
  2. Plus weakest regularity (continuity / monotonicity).
  3. Theorem (Cauchy): forces , nothing else.
  4. Convention: pick = pick your unit. Irrelevant downstream.
  5. Entropy = expected surprise = .

04Entropy: Definition, Shape, Units

Definition Entropy is the average surprise = the average information per outcome = the average number of binary digits to transmit the source under optimal coding. All the same expectation of the same per-outcome number .

"Expected amount of surprise" is the definition, not an analogy. A low-entropy source (e.g. 90% one outcome) is cheap to transmit; a high-entropy uniform source costs the full bits every time.

The non-uniform insight

When outcomes are unequal, give common things short codes and rare things long codes (Morse: 'E' = one dot, 'Q' = four symbols). The average number of questions per outcome under optimal coding is exactly the entropy. Information of one outcome = ; entropy = average over the distribution.

Shape for binary case (memorize)

For class proportion : . A symmetric dome: 0 at , peaks at 1.0 when , back to 0 at . Pure = no surprise = 0; 50/50 = max surprise = 1 bit.

Units recap

Nats = same entropy via (base ); 1 nat ≈ 1.44 bits. Used because has no stray — cleaner calculus, so ML losses default to natural log. Bits when the story is storage / transmission. Pure unit choice; rescales everything uniformly, changes no argmax or optimum.

05Cross-Entropy

Entropy assumed one known distribution. Cross-entropy splits it into truth vs. belief.

  • = the true distribution (reality, the actual labels).
  • = the model's predicted distribution.
Definition Average surprise you actually experience when reality is but you encoded expectations using . The log holds your belief ; the weighting out front is what truly happens .

Coding intuition (the "why we care" anchor)

Design a codebook optimized for — short codes for events you think are common. But events actually arrive per . Your average message length is . If beliefs were perfect () you'd hit the floor . Any mismatch costs extra bits, so always .

Where we measure it — the classifier loss

In supervised classification the true label is one-hot: , all mass on correct class . Plug in:

Every term dies except the correct class. So cross-entropy loss = negative log-probability assigned to the right answer. Confident-and-wrong is punished brutally ( as ) — exactly the gradient signal you want.

SettingHow CE appears
Image / tabular classificationOne CE per example over the class softmax.
LLM trainingThis is the objective. Per token: distribution over vocab; true label = actual next token (one-hot); loss , averaged over positions.
Perplexity — same number, exponentiated into "effective vocabulary size."
One-liner Minimizing cross-entropy = maximizing likelihood. summed over data is exactly the negative log-likelihood. "Train with cross-entropy" and "do MLE" are the same statement.

06KL Divergence

"Cross-entropy minus entropy" — the extra surprise from using the wrong distribution.

Definition KL = cross-entropy − entropy = the wasted bits. Cross-entropy is the total bill; entropy is the unavoidable floor; KL is the overpayment from your model being wrong.
PropertyDetail
Non-negative, iff (Gibbs' inequality — same Jensen argument as IG ≥ 0).
Asymmetric. Not a distance — frequent interview trap.
Forward KLMean-seeking / mass-covering.
Reverse KLMode-seeking. (Shows up in variational inference, RLHF.)
Why classifiers minimize CE, not KL depends only on fixed labels, not on — a constant during training. So : identical gradients. CE is just cheaper to compute, so that's what the loss says.

07MLE, Log-Loss & "Are We Optimizing Entropy?"

The confusion: the logistic / NN classification loss looks like entropy. It isn't — it's cross-entropy, and that resolves everything.

The formula you stare at

Logistic regression, one example, true label , predicted probability :

It resembles binary entropy . Look at the arguments:

Weight out frontInside the logHow many distributions
Entropy One (measures its own disorder)
Your loss (truth) (prediction)Two → cross-entropy
Resolution You are not optimizing the entropy of your predictions. You minimize the cross-entropy between true labels and predictions: , .

MLE produces exactly this

Bernoulli likelihood of one label: (check: ; ). Negative log:

That is the loss, verbatim. Negative log-likelihood and cross-entropy are the same expression — one derived from "maximize probability of data," the other from "match two distributions."

The decomposition that kills the confusion

Over the dataset, with the true labels and the predictions:

  • : entropy of the fixed labels — your weights can't change it. The irreducible noise floor.
  • : the only part parameters touch.
Direct answer The entropy of the predictions never appears. Minimizing the loss drives down : pushing your predicted distribution toward the true one. Minimize cross-entropy = minimize KL = MLE. Three names, one optimization.
Sanity check At the optimum with hard 0/1 labels and : cross-entropy loss , and the entropy of a hard (one-hot) label is also . Both pieces of the decomposition vanish — they snap together.

08Knowledge Distillation (KD) Loss

The payoff of the cross-entropy / KL machinery: it is "cross-entropy minus entropy," which is why KD belongs in this thread.

Setup & the insight

A big accurate teacher, a small cheap student you want to mimic it. Naive training uses hard one-hot labels. KD's insight: the teacher's full output distribution carries far more — the "dark knowledge."

Teacher sees a dog photo → {dog:0.9, wolf:0.08, cat:0.015, car:0.005}. The one-hot label only says "dog." The soft distribution says dog looks like wolf, a bit like cat, nothing like car — relational structure you teach the student.

The loss

There is the "cross-entropy minus entropy." Teacher frozen ⟹ constant ⟹ minimizing KD-KL = minimizing the cross-entropy between teacher and student. The student does CE training with the teacher's soft probabilities as "labels."

Temperature

Teacher probs are often too peaked (0.9 drowns the useful 0.08). Soften both with temperature :

Higher flattens the distribution, amplifying small-logit (dark-knowledge) signal. Train at high , deploy at . KD gradients scale as , so the KD term is multiplied by to stay comparable to the hard-label term.

Full objective in practice

Learn from ground truth and mimic the teacher.

When / whyUse case
Compression for deploymentShrink a giant model for phone / edge / low-latency serving — the dominant use case.
Small-model ecosystemMost "distilled" / small open-weight models (DistilBERT lineage, small variants of model families) are KD products — they beat same-size from-scratch models, since soft targets are richer signal.
Ensemble compressionDistill a 10-model ensemble into one network.
Label smoothing connectionA degenerate cousin: KD against a uniform "teacher."
Why teacher-first KL, not student-first? (forward, teacher first) is mass-covering: the student is penalized wherever the teacher puts mass but the student doesn't — it must reproduce the teacher's full distribution including the dark-knowledge tail. Reverse would be mode-seeking: the student could collapse onto the teacher's single peak and ignore the relational tail — defeating the point of distillation.
Unifying model Everything here is cross-entropy. Classifier training = CE against one-hot labels. Distillation = CE against a teacher's soft labels. KL = the part of CE your model can reduce, because the rest is a fixed floor.

09Gini Impurity & Its Link to Entropy

Same goal as entropy (measure mixedness), different imaginary game.

Derivation

Pick a random element (class w.p. ). Guess its label by sampling from the same distribution (guess w.p. ). Probability wrong for that element: . Averaged over the true class:

Meaning Gini = expected misclassification rate of the dumbest classifier (random guessing matched to class frequencies). Lower = purer. Range for binary: [0, 0.5], peak 0.5 at 50/50.

Two games measuring one disease

The game"Cost" it measures
EntropyIdentify the class via optimal yes/no questionsUncertainty / description length (bits)
GiniGuess the class by random proportional stab — how often wrong?Error probability

Both are 0 at a pure node, maximal at uniform, monotonic in between — that's why they agree on splits. "Hard to identify" and "easy to misclassify" are two symptoms of the same disease (mixedness).

Mathematical link

First-order Taylor of around : . Substitute into entropy:

So Gini is literally a first-order approximation of entropy — the reason the curves overlap and they pick the same split ~99% of the time.

Why Gini is the sensible default

  • Aligned with the task: a tree ultimately classifies and is judged on errors. Gini is already in the currency of classification error; entropy is in the currency of information (a slightly more roundabout proxy).
  • Speed: no logarithm — and impurity is computed millions of times across thresholds × features × nodes.
  • They rarely disagree, so the cheaper one costs nothing. When they do: entropy is marginally more sensitive near the pure end (log blows up faster as ), occasionally preferring more balanced splits. Academic for nearly all problems.

10Information Gain & Gini Gain

Impurity measures one node's mess. Gain is the decision rule — how much the split reduced average mess.

Symbols

SymbolMeaning
Set of samples at the current node (e.g. 100 emails, 60 spam / 40 not).
A candidate feature/question to split on (e.g. "contains the word 'free'?").
Entropy of the node before splitting.
Subset of samples whose feature takes value (e.g. , ).
Definition Measure mess before, measure mess after, subtract. Big drop ⟹ the question sorted samples into cleaner groups. Compute for every feature, split on the max.

The weighting matters (the trap)

The "after" term is a size-weighted average; weights are the fraction of samples in each branch (sum to 1). Worked example — 100 emails split on "contains 'free'?":

S_yes: 30 emails, entropy 0.3
S_no : 70 emails, entropy 0.9
after = (30/100)(0.3) + (70/100)(0.9) = 0.09 + 0.63 = 0.72
IG    = H(S) - 0.72 = 0.97 - 0.72 = 0.25 bits

If a split sent 990 samples into a still-90%-messy node and 10 into a pure node, an unweighted average would look decent — but almost all data is still messy. Size-weighting makes the big messy node dominate, so IG comes out low. Weighting stops a tiny clean offshoot from faking a good split.

Two readings (same arithmetic)

  • Operational: how much average impurity dropped after splitting on . (What you compute.)
  • Information-theoretic: the "after" term is , so = how much knowing reduces uncertainty about the label = the mutual information between feature and label. (The high-value interview answer to "what is IG fundamentally?")
IG ≥ 0 always (the trap) A real theorem, not a convention: from concavity of entropy via Jensen, conditioning never increases expected entropy, . Worst case the feature is useless ⟹ IG = 0. It can never go negative — the split can never increase average impurity.

Gini Gain — the exact analogue

The "gain" idea is impurity-measure-agnostic. Reuse the identical skeleton with Gini swapped in:

Same parent-minus-weighted-children structure; same greedy argmax procedure. CART (sklearn default) runs this with Gini. Note: IG has the clean mutual-information identity; Gini Gain is "reduction in expected proportional-guess error" — no equally elegant named identity.

11How a Decision Tree Is Built

Greedy recursive partitioning. "Recursive" = same procedure per node. "Greedy" = locally best split, no look-ahead.

BuildTree(samples S):
    1. Should I stop?  (stopping conditions) -> if yes, make S a LEAF, return.
    2. Otherwise, find the single best (feature, split-point) over ALL features.
    3. Split S into children by that test.
    4. Recurse: BuildTree on each child.

Step 2 — finding the best split

best_gain = 0; best_split = None
for each feature f:
    for each candidate split-point t on feature f:
        partition S into children using (f, t)
        gain = impurity(S) - weighted_impurity(children)
        if gain > best_gain:
            best_gain = gain; best_split = (f, t)

"Evaluate a split" = partition the samples, compute weighted child impurity, subtract from parent. The only open question is what the set of candidate split-points is per feature type — covered next.

12Splitting: Continuous & Categorical Features

Continuous — "won't there be infinite splits?"

Key insight Gain only changes when the threshold crosses an actual data point. Between consecutive values (say 28 and 35), every threshold (30, 31, 32.7…) yields the same partition → same gain. So test only one candidate per gap — the midpoint between adjacent distinct values. With samples: at most candidates. Finite, not infinite. Splits are always binary ("feature < t?").

Procedure: sort by the feature (), then candidate thresholds = midpoints between adjacent distinct values. Sorted [22, 28, 35, 40] → candidates {25, 31.5, 37.5}.

Speed optimizations

  • Incremental sweep: after sorting once, sweep left→right updating class counts (move one sample from right child to left at each step) instead of recomputing impurity — each threshold becomes ; the feature is , dominated by the sort.
  • Histogram binning (XGBoost / LightGBM): pre-bucket into e.g. 256 bins, only consider bin boundaries. Per-node cost depends on #bins, not . The single biggest reason modern boosted trees are fast on large tabular data.

Categorical — "won't permutations blow up?"

RegimeHow it splitsIssue
Multi-way (ID3 / C4.5)One branch per category valueNo combinatorial blowup, but high-cardinality features (ZIP, 40k values) make many tiny near-pure children → fake high IG. Fix: Information Gain Ratio = IG / split-info.
Binary subset (CART)Partition categories into two subsets possible partitions — the real blowup. → ~500k. Defused by Breiman's shortcut (see §17).

Escapes from the blowup: Breiman's sort-by-target shortcut (collapses to ) and target/ordinal encoding (CatBoost — convert category to a number, treat as continuous). Both detailed in §17.

13Stopping, Pruning & Leaf Predictions

When to stop expanding (make a leaf)

  • Pure node — all one class, impurity 0, nothing to gain.
  • No positive gain — best available IG ≈ 0; no feature separates further.
  • Pre-pruning / regularization limits (hyperparameters): max_depth, min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, min_impurity_decrease.
Why limits exist Without them a tree grows until every leaf is pure — a leaf per sample in the worst case — i.e. it memorizes the training data → severe overfitting. This is why a lone decision tree almost always overfits, and why these caps exist.

Pre- vs post-pruning

Pre-pruning (the limits above) is greedy and can stop too early — a weak split might enable a great split below it (the XOR problem). Post-pruning grows the tree fully then prunes branches that don't help validation (cost-complexity pruning / ccp_alpha in sklearn — penalizes tree size, snips subtrees whose accuracy gain doesn't justify complexity). Post-pruning avoids early stopping but costs more compute. In practice: pre-pruning limits + ensembling.

Leaf predictions

  • Classification: majority class of the leaf's samples (or class proportions for predicted probabilities).
  • Regression: mean of the leaf's target values (sometimes median).

14Classification → Regression Trees

Same skeleton; two things change.

Stays identical

The entire structure — greedy recursive partitioning, the candidate-split machinery (continuous sort-and-midpoint, categorical handling), stopping conditions, greedy argmax-over-splits loop.

What changes

  1. The impurity measure. Entropy/Gini measure class mixedness — meaningless for a continuous target. Replace with variance / MSE: node impurity = variance of its targets. The criterion becomes variance reduction: Same "parent − size-weighted children" formula, Var swapped for . (MAE is a robust-to-outliers but slower alternative.)
  2. The leaf prediction. Class (majority vote) → number (mean of leaf's targets).
Summary Same algorithm; swap "how I measure messiness" (entropy/Gini → variance) and "what I output" (vote → average). Everything about gain, weighting, splitting, stopping transfers directly.

15Complexity: Training & Inference

Notation: = number of training samples (rows), = number of features (columns).

Training

One node, samples: for each feature, sort () then sweep candidate thresholds incrementally. Across features: .

Sum over nodes — level by level. At any depth level, nodes partition the data into disjoint groups, so sample counts across siblings sum to . Sorting cost across one level is (the terms sum to ≤ since ). Multiply by depth = for a balanced tree:

Where the two logs come from (don't conflate) One = sorting samples per node. One = tree depth (number of levels).

Presort optimization: sort every feature once up front (), maintain order while partitioning instead of re-sorting per node. Per-level work becomes ; total for a balanced tree. So both (re-sort per node) and (presorted) appear as "the" answer — know which assumption each makes.

Worst case (degenerate tree, each split peels one sample, depth ): training — bounded in practice by depth / min-samples limits.

Inference

Walk one sample root→leaf, one comparison per node on the path:

The asymmetry (interview point) Inference depends on depth only — not on (you touch only the one feature each node tests, not all features) and not on (the tree is already built; training-set size doesn't appear). This is why tree ensembles are viable in latency-sensitive serving.
Balanced (typical)Worst case
Training (re-sort per node)
Training (presorted once)
Inference (per sample)
Space (store tree)

Space: #nodes ≤ since each leaf holds ≥ 1 sample — a tree can't have more leaves than samples.

16ID3 / C4.5 / CART & Library Reality

Three generations of decision-tree algorithms — a lineage.

AlgorithmCriterionSplitsNotes
ID3 (1986, Quinlan)Information Gain (entropy)Multi-way (one branch per value)Categorical only. No continuous, no missing, no pruning. IG biased toward high-cardinality features.
C4.5 (1993, Quinlan)Gain Ratio = IG / split-infoMulti-waySplit-info (entropy of partition sizes) penalizes many-way splits → fixes high-cardinality bias. Adds continuous, missing-value handling, post-pruning.
CART (1984, Breiman)Gini / variance reductionStrictly binaryParallel lineage (statistics community). Unifies classification + regression. Cost-complexity pruning, surrogate splits for missing.

Why binary (CART) beat multi-way (C4.5)

Multi-way splits fragment data fast — a 10-value feature spends all samples in one split and can't be reused. Binary splits are data-efficient, let you revisit a feature at multiple depths, and unify classification/regression. The cost — apparent blowup on categoricals — is defused by Breiman's theorem (§17).

What libraries actually use

LibraryWhat it is
sklearnOptimized CART — binary, Gini default (entropy optional). Does not natively handle categoricals — you must one-hot / ordinal-encode yourself.
XGBoostCART-style binary; own regularized gain (Taylor expansion of loss); histogram-based candidates; native categorical in recent versions.
LightGBMCART-style, histogram-based, leaf-wise growth; native categorical via Breiman's sort-by-target.
CatBoostCART-style oblivious (symmetric) trees; signature ordered target encoding for categoricals.
What to use Nobody ships ID3/C4.5 in production — the entire modern ecosystem is CART-derived. In practice: sklearn trees / RandomForest as baselines, XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost for performance.

17Categorical Tricks: Breiman & CatBoost

Two different tricks for the same problem: CART must split a categorical into two groups, and values give partitions.

Trick A — Breiman's theorem (sort-by-target shortcut)

For binary classification: compute each category's fraction of positive class, sort categories by it, treat the ordering like a continuous feature, and test only the contiguous cuts.

City% positive
Pune0.10
Delhi0.35
Chennai0.60
Mumbai0.85

Sorted: Pune → Delhi → Chennai → Mumbai. Test 3 contiguous cuts, not 7 subsets. For : 19 instead of ~500,000. Blowup → linear in , exactly like the continuous midpoint trick.

Why it's provably optimal The impurity-minimizing split groups categories that "behave the same" (similar positive-rate) together. Putting Pune (0.10) and Chennai (0.60) together while separating Delhi (0.35, which sits between them) is incoherent — it splits the middle while joining the extremes, always producing messier children. The optimal binary partition is always an interval in the positive-rate ordering, so contiguous cuts are the only candidates that can win.
The catch The clean guarantee holds only for binary classification and regression (a single scalar to sort by). For multiclass there's no single scalar — libraries fall back to heuristics. Common trap.

Trick B — CatBoost's ordered target encoding

CatBoost doesn't subset at all — it converts each category to a number (roughly the mean target for that category) then treats it as continuous.

The problem: target leakage Naive target encoding computes a category's value from all its rows including the current one — so a category appearing once is encoded as exactly its own label. The model "predicts" perfectly on training, catastrophically overfits, and the feature looks far more useful than it is.

The fix — "ordered" encoding: impose a random ordering on rows (artificial "time"); to encode row , use only rows before in that ordering — never row itself, never future rows:

where is a smoothing constant and the global prior (handles few/zero prior occurrences). Because each row sees only the past, its own label can never leak into its own feature. CatBoost averages over several random orderings to cut variance. This "look only at the past" principle is CatBoost's defining idea — applied to both target encoding and gradient computation.

A vs B — hold them apart

Breiman (LightGBM)CatBoost ordered encoding
What it doesKeeps category categorical; finds best subset split cheaply via sort-by-targetConverts category to a number, treats as continuous
SolvesThe combinatorial blowupHigh-cardinality categoricals + target leakage
Key ideaOptimal subset = interval in positive-rate orderEncode using only prior rows to prevent leakage
OutputA binary partition of categoriesA numeric feature

18Consolidated Q&A / Checks

The check questions raised through the session, gathered with their answers.

Q. Entropy of a node with 8 positives / 2 negatives — closer to 0 or 1?
bits. Closer to 0 than to 1, because the node is fairly pure (mostly positive) — less mixed than 50/50, so less surprise than the 1-bit maximum.
Q. Why is the surprise of two independent events the sum?
Information should accumulate linearly (two independent flips = twice the information of one, no overlap), and probabilities of independent events multiply — so we need a quantity mapping the multiplicative world of probability to the additive world of information. Concretely: storing two flips takes the digits of the first plus the digits of the second (you concatenate 1 then 0 → 10). Counts of concatenated things add. The log is what turns "probabilities multiply" into "storage adds."
Q. Why can't surprise be any function other than , and why no free constant?
Additivity + the mildest regularity is Cauchy's functional equation, whose only solutions are . The constant is free — but it's just a unit choice (bits vs nats), and it cancels out of every argmax and every gradient, so nothing downstream depends on it.
Q. In the KD objective, why (teacher first) and not the reverse?
Forward KL (teacher first) is mass-covering — it forces the student to reproduce the teacher's whole distribution including the dark-knowledge tail. Reverse KL is mode-seeking — the student could collapse onto the teacher's top peak and discard the relational tail, defeating distillation's purpose.
Q. Best-case split: 50 emails → 100% spam, 50 → 100% not-spam. What is IG?
Each child is pure → . Weighted "after" = . So — the maximum possible gain (the split removed all uncertainty).
Q. In a pure node, why is each game's cost zero — in words?
Entropy game: everything is one class, so you need zero yes/no questions to identify the label — you already know it. Gini game: your random proportional stab always draws the only class present, so you're never wrong — error rate 0.
Q. Sorted distinct values [10, 20, 50, 51] — how many candidate thresholds, and why is "< 35" vs "< 40" a waste?
3 thresholds — midpoints {15, 35, 50.5}. Both 35 and 40 fall in the same gap (between 20 and 50), so they produce the identical partition ({10,20} | {50,51}), identical impurity, identical gain — testing both is redundant.
Q. Why does inference complexity not depend on , though training does?
At inference you walk one root-to-leaf path, and at each node you test only the single feature that node splits on — never all features. Training must search all features at every node to choose the best split; inference just follows the already-chosen ones.
Q (trap). Why can't {Pune, Chennai} vs {Delhi, Mumbai} be the impurity-minimizing split?
In positive-rate order Pune(0.10) → Delhi(0.35) → Chennai(0.60) → Mumbai(0.85), this partition joins the extremes (Pune, Chennai) while separating Delhi which sits between them — splitting the middle and mixing dissimilar-rate categories on each side always yields messier children than a contiguous interval cut. The optimal subset is always an interval in this ordering.
Q (trap). Can Information Gain be negative?
No. always — conditioning never increases expected entropy (Jensen / concavity). Worst case the feature is useless → IG = 0.
Q. Why does a pure leaf have entropy 0?
, and all other ; the sum collapses to 0. Intuitively, zero surprise in a pure node.

★One-Page Formula Strip

Surprise / Information: (same number, before vs after the event)

Entropy: (expected surprise; binary peak = 1 bit at )

Cross-entropy: (one-hot label ⟹ = log-loss = NLL)

KL: , asymmetric

Decomposition: ⟹ minimize CE = minimize KL = MLE

KD loss:

Gini: (≈ ·entropy, first-order Taylor; binary peak 0.5)

Information Gain: mutual info

Variance reduction (regression):

Tree complexity: train (re-sort) or (presort); infer , independent of
